pattern: Adding 1 to an entire rectangle is a 2D range update, so marking only the four corners of an (n+1)x(n+1) difference grid and then sweeping a 2D prefix sum over it reconstructs every cell's final count without ever touching a cell more than a constant number of times.
hints: Adding 1 to a whole rectangle for every query, cell by cell, is wasteful -- there's a way to record each rectangle update in constant time and only 'pay' for it once at the end.

Build a helper grid one row and one column larger than the answer. For a query with corners (r1,c1)-(r2,c2), bump the helper at (r1,c1) by +1, and cancel that bump just past each edge: at (r1,c2+1), at (r2+1,c1), and undo both cancellations together at (r2+1,c2+1).

After every query has left its four marks, sweep the helper grid with a 2D running sum (each cell adds the running total from the cell above and the cell to the left, minus the overlap) -- the value that lands on each cell is exactly how many queries covered it.

edge_inputs.description: smallest possible grid, a single query covering its only cell
two overlapping rectangles on a small grid
the same rectangle requested three times, so overlap counts must stack correctly
many single-cell queries scattered across the grid rather than one big rectangle
a full single row and a full single column update
one query covering the entire grid
non-overlapping tiles that exactly partition a 4x4 grid into four quadrants

stress.description: near-maximum grid with thousands of random (validly ordered) rectangle queries
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = 500
    q = max(1, min(size, 10000))
    queries = []
    for _ in range(q):
        r1 = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        r2 = rng.randint(r1, n - 1)
        c1 = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        c2 = rng.randint(c1, n - 1)
        queries.append([r1, c1, r2, c2])
    return [n, queries]
